does: This paper introduces Persona-Conditioned Adversarial Prompting (PCAP), a red-teaming method that systematically discovers adversarial prompts by conditioning attack search on diverse personas and strategy sets. Rather than unconstrained optimization, PCAP runs parallel searches across personas like doctors, students, and malicious actors, exploring realistic attack scenarios grounded in different contexts. The method automatically tracks metadata for discovered attacks and produces 2–6× more diverse adversarial examples than baseline approaches. On GPT-OSS 120B, PCAP achieves 97% attack success rate compared to 57% baseline, and demonstrates a complete closed-loop pipeline: vulnerabilities discovered through persona-conditioned search are used to fine-tune lightweight adapters that substantially improve model robustness (F1 from 0.53 to 0.96) while maintaining minimal false positives.
connects: This work extends automated red-teaming for large language models, building on prior adversarial discovery methods that often suffer from narrow attack coverage and insufficient diversity for robust safety training. PCAP responds to limitations in unconstrained adversarial prompting by incorporating structured scenario exploration inspired by red-teaming practices in security and threat modeling. The approach combines ideas from multi-objective optimization—exploring diverse attack vectors simultaneously—with insights from controllable text generation and fine-grained conditioning. It also builds on existing work in safety fine-tuning and adversarial training by providing a systematic mechanism to generate the diverse, well-annotated datasets needed for effective alignment, addressing a key bottleneck in moving from vulnerability discovery to practical defense.
enables: This work establishes a scalable framework for continuous safety improvement that could be applied across diverse LLM architectures and deployment contexts. By conditioning red-teaming on realistic personas, the method generates data reflecting actual threat distributions, enabling more targeted and effective defenses than generic adversarial training. The automatic metadata tracking of discovered vulnerabilities creates rich resources for understanding model failure modes and designing persona-aware safety mechanisms. Future work could extend PCAP to hierarchical persona taxonomies, adversarial adaptation (where personas co-evolve with defenses), and cross-model transfer of jailbreaks and defenses. This foundation also supports developing personalized safety policies—different behavioral constraints for different user contexts—and understanding which vulnerability patterns generalize across models versus remain architecture-specific.
